---
title: "Week 5 Answers (0324)"
---

## Can your Quiz score predict your Midterm score?

In this practice, we will use the (simulated) data from the 2025 Statistics with Recitation course to examine whether students' quiz scores can be good predictors of their midterm scores.

::: {.callout-tip title="Note"}
This dataset is not the raw data from the course. I simulated the data based on the raw data and made sure that the new dataset generates results very similar to those of the original dataset.
:::

The dataset contains:

- `ID`: The ID number of each student.
- `section`: The section each student belongs to.
- `midterm`, `quiz_1`, `quiz_2`, `quiz_3`: The corresponding midterm and quiz scores, where midterm scores range from 0 to 100 and quiz scores range from 0 to 30.

Please follow the instructions below to conduct the analysis. (Take a guess before you do it!)


0. Before you begin, complete the setup:

   - Open the project you created for the TA Session by clicking on `.Rproj` (if you do not have one, create it).
   - Open a new script and save in the script folder.
   - Download the dataset `grades.csv` and save it in the `data` folder. [(right click here)](grades.csv){target=_blank rel="noopener"}  
   - Call the package `tidyverse`.
   - Create an `output` folder inside the project folder if you do not have one.
   - (Optional) Remove all objects from the environment using `rm(list = ls())`.

1. Read `grades.csv` and save it as `grades_raw`.

2. Replace all blank cells in `grades_raw` with `NA`.

::: {.callout-tip title="Hint"}
Use `grades_raw[grades_raw == ""] <- NA`.
:::

3. Create a new dataframe called `grades_tidy` from `grades_raw` with the following steps:

   - Keep only the rows where `section` is `"國立臺灣大學修課學生 (ECON2022-03)"`.
   - Remove the columns `ID` and `section`.
   - Remove rows with missing values using `na.omit()`.
   - Convert `midterm`, `quiz_1`, `quiz_2`, and `quiz_3` to numeric type.
   - Create a new column `avg_quiz_score` equal to the average of `quiz_1`, `quiz_2`, and `quiz_3`.

4. Rename the columns of `grades_tidy` as follows: `midterm`, `quiz_ch_1to2`, `quiz_ch_3to4`, `quiz_r`,  `avg_quiz_score`.

5. First, let's see if there's relationship between students' midterm scores and their average quiz scores. Make a scatter plot with `avg_quiz_score` on the x-axis, `midterm` on the y-axis, and with the settings:

   - x-axis: `"Average Quiz Score"`, ranges from `0` to `30`
   - y-axis: `"Midterm Score"`, ranges from `0` to `100`
   - color: `"steelblue"`
   - size: `2.5`
   - Labels: x `"Average Quiz Score"`, y `"Midterm Score"`

6. Print the correlation between `midterm` and `avg_quiz_score`.

7. Fit a linear regression model called `reg_midterm_on_avg` with `midterm` as the response variable and `avg_quiz_score` as the predictor. Save its summary as `reg_midterm_on_avg_summary`, and print the summary. What is the result? Does it match what you see in the scatter plot?

8. Next, let us investigate further to see which quiz serves as the best predictor of the midterm score. Fit a linear regression model called `reg_midterm_on_all_quiz` with `midterm` as the response variable and `quiz_ch_1to2`, `quiz_ch_3to4`, and `quiz_r` as the predictors. Save its summary as `reg_midterm_on_all_quiz_summary`, and print the summary. **How would you explain the regression result?**

9. Creating a plot `reg_midterm_on_avg_plot` by adding a regression line (in step 6) to the plot you just created in step 5 with the following settings and then print it.

   - slope: the coefficient of `avg_quiz_score` from `reg_midterm_on_avg_summary`
   - intercept: the intercept from `reg_midterm_on_avg_summary`
   - color: `"lightcoral"`
   - linetype: `"dashed"`

10. Save `reg_midterm_on_avg_plot` into an `output` folder as a `png` file named `reg_midterm_on_avg_plot.png` using `ggsave()` with the settings: `width = 12`, `height = 8`, `dpi = 300`.

``` r

rm(list = ls())

library(tidyverse)

grades_raw <- read_csv("data/grades.csv") 

grades_raw[grades_raw == ""] <- NA

grades_tidy <- grades_raw %>% 
  filter(section == "國立臺灣大學修課學生 (ECON2022-03)") %>%
  select(-c(ID, section)) %>%
  na.omit() %>%
  mutate(midterm = as.numeric(midterm)) %>%
  mutate(quiz_1 = as.numeric(quiz_1)) %>%
  mutate(quiz_2 = as.numeric(quiz_2)) %>%
  mutate(quiz_3 = as.numeric(quiz_3)) %>%
  mutate(avg_quiz_score = (quiz_1 + quiz_2 + quiz_3) / 3)

colnames(grades_tidy) <- c("midterm", "quiz_ch_1to2", "quiz_ch_3to4", "quiz_r", "avg_quiz_score")

ggplot(grades_tidy, aes(x = avg_quiz_score, y = midterm)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Average Quiz Score",
    y = "Midterm Score"
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 30)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100))
  
cor(grades_tidy$midterm, grades_tidy$avg_quiz_score)

reg_midterm_on_avg <- lm(midterm ~ avg_quiz_score, data = grades_tidy)
reg_midterm_on_avg_summary <- summary(reg_midterm_on_avg)
print(reg_midterm_on_avg_summary)

reg_midterm_on_all_quiz <- lm(midterm ~ quiz_ch_1to2 + quiz_ch_3to4 + quiz_r, data = grades_tidy)
reg_midterm_on_all_quiz_summary <- summary(reg_midterm_on_all_quiz)
print(reg_midterm_on_all_quiz_summary)

reg_midterm_on_avg_plot <- ggplot(grades_tidy, aes(x = avg_quiz_score, y = midterm)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Average Quiz Score",
    y = "Midterm Score"
    ) + 
  geom_abline(
    slope = reg_midterm_on_avg_summary$coefficients[2],
    intercept = reg_midterm_on_avg_summary$coefficients[1],
    color = "lightcoral",
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 30)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100))

print(reg_midterm_on_avg_plot)

ggsave(filename = "reg_midterm_on_avg_plot.png",
       plot = reg_midterm_on_avg_plot,
       path = "output",
       width = 12,
       height = 8,
       dpi = 300)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false      

rm(list = ls())

library(tidyverse)
library(here)

grades_raw <- read.csv(here("data", "grades.csv")) 

grades_raw[grades_raw == ""] <- NA

grades_tidy <- grades_raw %>% 
  filter(section == "國立臺灣大學修課學生 (ECON2022-03)") %>%
  select(-c(ID, section)) %>%
  na.omit() %>%
  mutate(midterm = as.numeric(midterm)) %>%
  mutate(quiz_1 = as.numeric(quiz_1)) %>%
  mutate(quiz_2 = as.numeric(quiz_2)) %>%
  mutate(quiz_3 = as.numeric(quiz_3)) %>%
  mutate(avg_quiz_score = (quiz_1 + quiz_2 + quiz_3) / 3)

colnames(grades_tidy) <- c("midterm", "quiz_ch_1to2", "quiz_ch_3to4", "quiz_r", "avg_quiz_score")

ggplot(grades_tidy, aes(x = avg_quiz_score, y = midterm)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Average Quiz Score",
    y = "Midterm Score"
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 30)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100))

cor(grades_tidy$midterm, grades_tidy$avg_quiz_score)

reg_midterm_on_avg <- lm(midterm ~ avg_quiz_score, data = grades_tidy)
reg_midterm_on_avg_summary <- summary(reg_midterm_on_avg)
print(reg_midterm_on_avg_summary)


reg_midterm_on_all_quiz <- lm(midterm ~ quiz_ch_1to2 + quiz_ch_3to4 + quiz_r, data = grades_tidy)
reg_midterm_on_all_quiz_summary <- summary(reg_midterm_on_all_quiz)
print(reg_midterm_on_all_quiz_summary)

reg_midterm_on_avg_plot <- ggplot(grades_tidy, aes(x = avg_quiz_score, y = midterm)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Average Quiz Score",
    y = "Midterm Score"
    ) + 
  geom_abline(
    slope = reg_midterm_on_avg_summary$coefficients[2],
    intercept = reg_midterm_on_avg_summary$coefficients[1],
    color = "lightcoral",
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 30)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100))

print(reg_midterm_on_avg_plot)

ggsave(filename = "reg_midterm_on_avg_plot.png",
       plot = reg_midterm_on_avg_plot,
       path = here("output"),
       width = 12,
       height = 8,
       dpi = 300)
```


![](reset.gif){width="960" fig-align="center" fig-alt="Animated welcome"}
